meta.name: draft
meta.description: DRAFT 워크플로 — 아이디어(directive)를 백로그 덩어리로 구체화. generate=요건 발굴·평탄 발행, 항목 검증=exec-one, 누락=hunt, 분류=classify(완성 집합), 완결 인증=audit.

values.COMMON:
SHARED CONCEPTS:
- A REQUIREMENT = an imperative the result must satisfy ("the system/plan/novel/work must …"): concrete and developable/executable — NOT a background fact, NOT a restatement of the directive, NOT a bare umbrella ("manage/handle/support X" that names no specific operation or obligation). (Form: not "X regulations" but "the system must DO <Y> to satisfy <X>".)
- MAKE-OR-BREAK = its absence would make the result FAIL or be WRONG, not merely less polished. A genuine one is a DECISION two competent practitioners could resolve DIFFERENTLY — NOT a nice-to-have, NOT one methodology's enumerated beat-list, NOT the HOW / implementation-detail of another requirement (that is covered by its parent, not a separate requirement).
- EXPERT STANCE: read THIS directive as the SENIOR PRACTITIONER of its real domain (a pharmacist / compliance officer for a drug system, a novelist for a novel, an expedition leader for a climb). An expert never stops at the CATEGORY — "comply with the narcotics law" / "protect personal data" is NOT a requirement; it HIDES the concrete obligations the law compels, each its own make-or-break ("on a stock discrepancy, file the incident report to the authority within the statutory deadline"; "verify the vendor is a licensed wholesaler at registration"). Name the SPECIFIC trigger / deadline / check whoever builds, writes, or executes it must satisfy — a distinct requirement an expert knows, never an implementation beat. (Likewise outside law: a novel — not "a satisfying ending" but the specific turn that earns it; a plan — not "be safe" but the specific abort threshold.) A broad "support / comply with X" topic HIDES the gap, it does not cover it.
- THE BACK-SIDE: the requester is NOT a domain expert — they named the visible SURFACE (the easy 80%) and, even in a DETAILED directive, omit the make-or-break BACK-SIDE (the 20% that decides success) a senior practitioner / law / safety requires for the intent to actually work, be legal, be safe (the administrative, legal, financial, safety, contingency/failure-handling, oversight/who-administers substrate). DRAW IT OUT — adversarially ask of THIS intent: who actually OPERATES it, OVERSEES/administers it, PAYS FOR it, is kept SAFE/legal by it, and RECOVERS it when it fails or ends — and what does each REQUIRE that the requester never said? Don't be seduced by a polished, plausible surface: that polish IS the 80% trap. Then use the per-domain SHAPES below — the KIND to hunt; they COMPLEMENT the questions above (a minimal domain hint), never REPLACE them, and are NOT answers (search the real content; apply ONLY what genuinely fits THIS directive, never force a non-applicable category):
    · SYSTEM → operator/admin console per permission grade & oversight, data model, regulation (the SPECIFIC reporting/incident triggers, deadlines, and qualifications the governing law compels — kinds to hunt, not answers), security boundaries, monitoring, lifecycle/offboarding.
    · NOVEL → the avenger's corrosion, justice-vs-vengeance, antagonist depth, the delay engine, the payoff, the aftermath, POV/reveal-order, setting/world, reader complicity.
    · PLAN → go/no-go gates, per-step verification, contingency/rollback, failure modes, legal/safety preconditions (the SPECIFIC approvals, clearances, and qualifications required before an act may proceed — kinds to hunt, not answers), responsibility, exit criteria.
    · EVERYDAY (e.g. moving house) → registration, deposit/fee settlement, address changes, defect-check — not just the visible act.
- THE ACTORS: a directive implies every actor the result touches, yet the requester writes as only one of them. The actors they never played still carry make-or-breaks. Every built thing has, without exception, both on-stage actors, who use the result, and off-stage actors, who run it — the parties that operate, moderate, deploy, and answer for it. The off-stage actors are part of the system itself, never an afterthought. In a web or SaaS service, the off-stage actor is the platform operator, who suspends violating tenants and enforces subscription entitlements. In a game, it is live-ops and moderation, who tune the economy, handle bans and appeals, and roll back incidents. In a desktop or mobile app, it is the deployer, who owns the update channel, triages crashes, and enforces licenses. In an internal system, it is the administrator, who provisions accounts, keeps the audit trail, and rehearses recovery. A third archetype the on-stage/off-stage split misses: EXTERNAL COUNTERPARTIES, who transact THROUGH the result from outside it — by domain, the vendor or supplier who fulfills what users request, the partner who integrates, the external reviewer who signs off — a SEPARATE PARTY with their own surface, not a permission grade of the users' app, their make-or-breaks as load-bearing as any user's. And every distinct actor the directive implies reaches the result through its OWN surface — draw those surfaces as requirements (OPERATING SURFACES below), not only the scattered capabilities behind them. Example requirements from an off-stage actor: "the operator must be able to suspend a violating tenant and revoke its active sessions"; "when a subscription expires, the system must downgrade that tenant's entitlements automatically — the system enforces this, it is not left to good faith."
- OPERATING SURFACES: capabilities are not yet a usable system — walk the OPERATING reality and draw the surfaces the result is actually RUN through, each its own requirement. A capability reachable only through a back-end service, with no surface any actor operates it through, is a MISSING requirement — the surface IS the requirement, not its implementation. Walk them by kind: how each actor is VERIFIABLY authenticated and bound to its role/grade for the session (authentication) — the foundation the whole permission model rests on: without a forgery-proof identity-to-role basis, every permission, access rule, and per-grade console is unenforceable and any caller can claim any role, so wherever roles/grades/permissions exist, authentication is a make-or-break, not an afterthought, how requests reach the capabilities at all (entrypoint / routing), the console each distinct actor and each permission-grade operates through (a per-grade admin console is its own deliverable, NOT implied by the permission model — "can do X" is not "the screen where X is done"), the screens that read the ledgers / evidence / audit records, what must run on a schedule (jobs / batch), and how the pieces boot and wire together. The test: if the assembled result could be BUILT but not OPERATED — no actor could actually DO the thing — a surface is missing, and that surface is the requirement. Necessity, not mention: draw only surfaces the result cannot be operated without; never pad with polish.
- LEGAL LENS: wherever the intent's success turns on real-world LAW, RULE, or LEGITIMACY — to be COMPLIANT (a statutory duty it must satisfy), to be PERMITTED (an approval / license / qualification that gates an act or a participation), or to be ACCURATE (a work that portrays or relies on real law) — surface the binding obligations, approvals, triggers, and deadlines the real, current law actually compels, not just the functional surface (ground them by GROUNDING below). This is NOT only for regulated systems: a plan may need a clearance, a novel may need its law right. Apply ONLY where the intent genuinely turns on law; never force it onto one that does not.
- GROUNDING (when to SEARCH vs REASON — the one rule for any fact you rely on): the real test is "could you be WRONG from memory?" — info beyond your knowledge cutoff (a recent event, the CURRENT status of a law/program/standard), OR the SPECIFICS of a named statute/article/standard/figure/framework you could misremember, OR genuine uncertainty → WebSearch (put the current year in queries; NEVER assert such specifics from memory). A general principle or common design/craft choice you RELIABLY know → reason it; do NOT search what you reliably know (wasteful), and never re-search the settled.
- COMMON-SENSE DEFAULT: where the directive leaves a gap or ambiguity, resolve it with the SIMPLEST answer common reasoning reaches — what most competent people would call obvious. Do NOT invent an unusual, elaborate, or restrictive mechanism (a quantity cap, an enforcement, a control) the directive never asked for; a plain reading beats a clever one. The unusual must come from the directive — never from you.
- INVARIANTS — every requirement, whether GENERATED or ADDED: (1) ATOMIC — one subject, not bundled, not over-split; (2) NO DUPLICATE — not a restatement of another, judged by MEANING not wording (a narrower / re-angled / renamed / split version of an existing one is NOT new); (3) NO FORCING/FABRICATION — a genuine grounded make-or-break, never invented to seem thorough.

concat:
YOUR ROLE — REFUTER (adversarial). Your job is NOT to confirm this requirement — it is to REFUTE it. Attempt to prove it is NOT a real make-or-break: wrong, unnecessary, out-of-scope, a duty the directive disclaims, or a bare umbrella naming no specific operation/obligation.

REQUIREMENT:
- {{title}} — {{description}}

Pick the method by GROUNDING (SHARED CONCEPTS): could you be WRONG from memory → WebSearch the specific and try to disprove (verified_value = fact + source); reliably know it → reason whether necessary AND sound.
Then judge (field "oxf"):
- Attempted to refute and COULD NOT — a real requirement → "o" + origin + verified_value + sources + reason.
- Refuted it, OR UNCERTAIN whether it holds → "x" + reason. Reject-if-uncertain: a shaky requirement carried on is worse than a real one dropped (hunt's rotating lenses re-surface a load-bearing one). A minor legitimate off, not a failure.
- Could not run the check (needed search errored/empty) → "f" + reason (retry, do not guess).
FINAL message = ONLY this JSON.

effort.description: 이 요건의 검증 난이도 tier(선택). 강한 신호 하나로 판정하고 평균으로 희석하지 마라 — 순수 기계적·되돌리기 쉬운 검증만 low/medium, 아키텍처·보안·프로덕션 위험이 걸린 검증은 high/max — 규제·법정 의무·불가역 데이터 무결성은 max. 애매하면 높게. 생략 시 최고. (routing-skill.md)

prompts.gen:
{{COMMON}}

YOUR ROLE — GENERATOR: turn the directive into a BACKLOG CHUNK (덩어리) — a title for the whole + the full FLAT set of REQUIREMENTS. **You do NOT classify.** Categorization is a LATER review step (classify), run after the set is complete; here you only DISCOVER.

1) CHUNK TITLE: if the directive states or clearly implies a name for the whole, EXTRACT it (titleOrigin "user"); else GENERATE a short faithful title from its real intent (titleOrigin "agent"). One short noun phrase in the directive's language — the name a practitioner files this backlog under.
2) REQUIREMENTS — **INTERPRET, do NOT echo.** Read the directive's real intent; never pass surface phrasing through verbatim. A terse directive bundles several DISTINCT requirements in one run-on clause — split each into its OWN atomic item (title = the imperative requirement in 입력 언어, description = one line of what it must do and why it is make-or-break, never a restatement of the title). ATOMIC: split bundled DISTINCT requirements; never split ONE requirement into its implementation beats. DECIDE, do NOT defer — a requirement STATES a concrete rule the build must satisfy, never a mandate to decide it later. FORBIDDEN: 'define whether X or Y', 'determine the policy for Z', 'decide when to …' — these leave the make-or-break decision OPEN and are not requirements. Where the directive leaves a choice open, MAKE the call and state it as the rule (not 'define whether an unapproved edit is held or applied' but 'an unapproved edit does not change the baseline stock'). A requirement whose content is 'a decision will be made' is a deferral; state the decision itself.
   **GENERATION IS GENEROUS — cast WIDE.** Include EVERY plausible make-or-break (content, structural/craft, operational, regulated, the back-side, and every actor — on-stage users, external counterparties who transact through it, and above all the off-stage actor who runs the result, a part the requester has never played — each drawn through its OWN named surface, not only its scattered functions). AND every DATA ENTITY the result stores, counts, or edits (each noun the directive treats as kept or managed — not only the ones whose creation it spells out) needs its OWN create / enter / edit / list surface: a rule ABOUT an entity (its constraint, its price, its identity, how it is classified) is NOT the surface that CREATES and maintains that entity. These entity-entry surfaces — above all the CENTRAL entity every other requirement revolves around — are the MOST-MISSED requirements, dropped because they seem too obvious to state; yet the result cannot be operated without them, and an entity's own registration/entry screen is owed as much as any flow or rule. Generosity is SAFE: the per-item verifier grounds each and rejects (x) any that does not hold — better to slightly OVER-include than to miss one. No cap, no stinginess; this set must be COMPLETE. Obey the INVARIANTS. origin = "user" if the directive states/implies it, "agent" if you derive it as a make-or-break the directive never stated. You cannot search here. There is NO optional tier — a nice-to-have is NOT a requirement.
   **DO NOT group, do NOT invent a category dimension, do NOT prune to fit a frame.** Pre-classifying prunes topics outside the frame and breaks completeness — emit requirements[] FLAT. The classify step (after hunt completes the set) invents the dimension and assigns categories then.

Directive: "{{directive}}"
prompts.classify:
{{COMMON}}

YOUR ROLE — CLASSIFIER (검토·분류). 생성·누락탐색이 끝났다 — 아래 원장이 **완성된 전체 요건**이다. 새 요건을 만들지 마라(그건 hunt 몫). 전체를 senior practitioner 로서 읽고, 이 도메인에 맞는 분류 차원 하나를 INVENT 한 뒤, 모든 요건을 정확히 한 카테고리로 배정한다.
  - DIMENSION: 이 도메인에 맞는 차원 하나를 발명(a system: 기능 영역; a novel: 막/장; a plan: 국면; a climb: 구간 — 고정 분류법 금지, directive 와 실제 요건들에서 도출). **집합이 이미 완성됐으니(hunt 후) 프레임 밖 토픽이 잘릴 위험이 없다 — 그래서 생성이 아니라 여기서 분류한다.**
  - ASSIGN: 원장의 모든 항목을 그 차원의 카테고리로. assignments[] 각 원소 = {id(원장 줄 맨 앞 [id]), category}. 모든 id 를 정확히 한 번씩, 정확히 한 카테고리로. category 는 짧은 명사구(입력 언어). 카테고리 수는 집합이 정하게 — 억지로 늘리거나 줄이지 마라.

완성된 전체 원장(각 줄 맨 앞 [id] 로 배정):
{{ledger}}

Directive: "{{directive}}"

FINAL message = ONLY this JSON.
prompts.audit:
{{COMMON}}

YOUR ROLE — AUDITOR (the backlog chunk's parent certification). The per-item badges are already set. Your job is NOT to re-judge items one by one — CERTIFY THE WHOLE ASSEMBLED SET (부품 아닌 전체): does this set, taken together, deliver the directive's goal completely and coherently? Judge four axes, give ONE verdict:
  - 누락 (gaps): any make-or-break MISSING that the goal cannot stand without? List each (empty if none).
  - 모순 (contradictions): any two requirements conflict so a builder is blocked until one is overruled? List each (empty if none).
  - 충분 (sufficiency): assembled, does the set REACH the goal — not just cover the surface? State plainly whether it suffices and why.
  - 운영 견고성 (operational robustness): 조립된 set 이 실제 운영을 견디나 — 동일 상태에 대한 동시/충돌 입력의 승자·불일치 조정, 규모에서의 findability(다수 중 하나 찾기), 의존 단계 부분실패의 폴백, 역할→보호동작 경계가 요건에 있나? happy-path·demo 규모·전부 성공 가정에만 서면 미완. 빠진 것 나열(empty if none).
Set complete=true ONLY if no goal-breaking gaps, no unresolved contradictions, and the set genuinely suffices. verdict = one paragraph: what the assembled draft achieves and the single most important thing still missing or wrong (or "완결" if none). Do NOT pad. Stay inside the directive's stated scope.

gaps is ALWAYS present: empty array when complete=true, one entry per missing make-or-break when false. A complete=false verdict REQUIRES gaps: every missing make-or-break becomes its own gaps entry — one line, the requirement the set lacks. Prose that describes the lack while leaving gaps empty is a broken deliverable; the correction loop can only act on gaps entries, never on prose.

REMOVE (removals) — self-correction, not only addition: the loop must also eject what should not be there, or a human has to delete it by hand. Scan the ledger for items that are refuted/wrong, redundant with another item, or outside the directive's stated scope. Each such item = one removals entry {"id": the exact ledger id, "reason": why it goes}. Do NOT remove an item because it is small or you would word it differently — remove ONLY wrong/redundant/out-of-scope. Items already badged x were removed in a prior round (reason shown in the ledger) — do NOT re-flag them and do NOT re-add them as gaps. removals is ALWAYS present: empty array when nothing currently in the set is wrong. Consensus = a round with no gaps AND no removals.

Full ledger (CERTIFY this whole):
{{ledger}}

Directive: "{{directive}}"

Do any needed search first. FINAL message = ONLY this JSON.
prompts.draft-review:
{{COMMON}}

YOUR ROLE — CONSENSUS REVIEWER (완전성 합의 루프, round {{round}} of max 20).

You are given a DOCUMENT: a JSON list of items, each with a `state` (o=accepted, x=removed) and a full decision `history` (add/remove/reraise, each with its reason). Deliberate against it and output CHANGES; the loop repeats until a round makes no change (consensus).

THE DOCUMENT (items with history):
{{document}}

Directive: "{{directive}}"

DELIBERATE over the requirements, then output changes[]:
- ADD (op:add, title, description, reason) — every make-or-break still MISSING. Lenses: goal-reach · contradiction · seam(경계 소유) · depth(regulated → the SPECIFIC obligation) · domain-failure(what breaks in real use) · actor-sweep(every actor incl. the off-stage operator, each with its OWN named surface) · operational robustness(concurrency·scale/findability·partial failure·role boundary) · INPUT/ENTITY-CRUD(every stored data entity needs create/enter/edit/list; the central entity's registration is the most-missed — too obvious to state). CONCRETIZATION (mention is not completeness) — for every distinctive thing the directive spells out (an unusual unit, a special-case rule, a non-default value, an exception), check it is OPERATIONALIZED, not merely named as an attribute. If the directive says a value 'can be set to 1/12', capturing 'a unit field' is HALF — that value ripples into every operation it touches (counting, deduction, display, reconciliation, validation), and those must be present too. The small, obvious-looking rules the directive bothered to specify carry the biggest ripples. A named-but-unrealized mechanic is a gap to fill, not a covered requirement. BIAS TOWARD COMPLETENESS: when borderline load-bearing, ADD it — over-reach is corrected by a later remove; a gap left out may never be found (allowing borderline adds is what gives the loop something to prune).
- REMOVE (op:remove, id, reason) — any accepted [o] item that is refuted/wrong, redundant with another BY MEANING, or outside the directive's scope. State why in reason.
- RERAISE (op:reraise, id, reason) — a removed [x] item may be brought back ONLY with a reason that directly COUNTERS the reason it was removed (in its history). Re-raising on already-countered grounds is oscillation — forbidden.

EVERY change MUST carry its reason (근거) — it is appended to the item's history. CONVERGENCE DISCIPLINE: this round must FINISH it — surface EVERY remaining change at once, do not dribble one or two per round; if you are past round 1, prior rounds left gaps, close them ALL now. If the NEXT round still changes what you touched, you mis-judged. When the set is complete and correct, output an EMPTY changes array — that is consensus. Do any needed search first.

FINAL message = ONLY {"changes":[...]}.
